title: Wavelength-specific urban nighttime light modulates expressed sentiment across China

data_scope.spatial_coverage: nationwide China, multi-city with within- and between-city mapping
data_scope.key_effect_sizes: blue ALAN +15.9% negative sentiment; green ALAN +5.0% positive sentiment; CCT optimization -89.7% sentiment risk vs intensity reduction
data_scope.time_window: not explicitly stated; tied to SDGSAT-1 era (launched 2021-11) and contemporaneous Weibo record

core_conclusion: Nighttime light affects mood through its spectrum, not just brightness. Coupling SDGSAT-1 multi-band nightlight with geotagged Weibo sentiment across China, blue light (424-526 nm) raises negative sentiment by 15.9% while moderate green light (506-612 nm) raises positive sentiment by 5.0%; sentiment risk concentrates in commercial areas and eastern cities, and optimizing correlated color temperature cuts sentiment risk by 89.7% versus dimming alone, arguing for spectral lighting design to protect public mental wellbeing.
why_it_matters: Reframes light pollution from brightness to wavelength; gives planners a cheaper lever (CCT tuning) than darkness; maps night-environment inequity in commercial and eastern cities; demonstrates a scalable satellite-plus-social-media pipeline for environment-mood links.
why_nature: Quantified wavelength-resolved dose-response at national scale; couples remote sensing with human behaviour; directly policy-translatable lighting-design prescription; surfaces within- and between-city equity disparities.
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable MGWR/SHAP partition of spectral ALAN vs pollution -> sharper confounded health-exposure attribution
ALAN sentiment-risk surface as demand-side weight in 2SFCA/R2SFCA mental-health accessibility
Fuse nightlight spectrum + greenness + pollution into one explainable wellbeing index
Social-media sentiment validated as a calibrated sensor -> reproducible multi-source data fusion
CCT retrofit as efficiency-vs-equity allocation -> reuse of my EV-charging optimization machinery
